text: Here is the AI panel opening its deliberation on whether a Colorado county can sue fossil fuel giants in state court.

That's the Textualist, moments before the rest of the room jumps in. Welcome to SPLIT DECISION, where we listen in on an artificial intelligence jury deliberating real Supreme Court cases. I'm your veteran court-watcher, reading the room and calling the flips before they land.

And I'm your legal-affairs correspondent, here to translate the law into plain English. Today's case is Suncor Energy versus Boulder County. Let's break down the facts and the human stakes. Boulder County and the City of Boulder sued Exxon Mobil and Suncor Energy. The county says these companies spent decades producing, refining, and selling fossil fuels, knowing full well it would drive climate change. They also say the companies intentionally misled the public about the dangers. Because of this, Boulder says it has suffered concrete, expensive harm. We are talking about real money spent on flood damage, increased wildfire risks, severe drought, and physical damage to public buildings and infrastructure. Crucially, Boulder is not asking the court to shut down the companies or regulate emissions. They just want the companies to pay for the damage they've already caused and will continue to cause. Boulder filed this traditional state lawsuit in state court.

And the fossil fuel companies immediately tried to move the case to federal court. They argue that federal law, specifically the Clean Air Act, blocks state courts from hearing any lawsuits about greenhouse gas emissions. They say the federal government has occupied the entire field, so state judges have no business here. If the companies win, state courts are locked out of climate damage lawsuits entirely. If Boulder wins, they can keep suing for damages in their home courts. The case bounced around the lower courts for years. It went up to the federal appeals court, then back down, and eventually landed in the Colorado Supreme Court. The state's highest court sided with Boulder, saying the state lawsuit can proceed. Now, the AI panel is looking at whether the state court actually has the legal right to hear this dispute.
The panel calls the room to order and takes their initial private votes on the core questions of court authority and federal law.

Right out of the gate, the room is unified. Every single jurist voted to keep the case in state court. No one thinks federal law automatically blocks this state lawsuit. The initial read of the room shows a strong instinct to let the state judges do their jobs.
The debate then shifts to how to handle the federal law argument without getting bogged down in endless policy fights. The Process Formalist and others are trying to keep the focus narrow.

You can hear the Minimalist and the others trying to rein in the room. They want to keep this strictly to the facts at hand. They are basically saying, just decide this specific case about Boulder County, do not write a massive, sweeping rule that dictates how the next fifty climate lawsuits will play out. They want to avoid a giant clash over the Clean Air Act today.
The debate kicks off over whether they must decide the basic rules of court authority first, or just look at the big picture of interstate commerce and how state laws affect it.

In plain English, they are arguing over whether to follow the strict rulebook for court power first, or just look at the practical mess of having fifty different states making their own climate rules. The Pragmatist is worried that letting every state sue will create an unworkable patchwork that paralyzes interstate business. The Process Formalist keeps pulling them back to the basic threshold questions of who has the power to hear the case.
The Precedent Maximalist pushes back on those fears about a messy patchwork of state rules, pointing to older pollution cases to show that courts can handle this.

The Maximalist is basically telling the Pragmatist to calm down. They are pointing out that courts have handled complex, multi-state pollution lawsuits before, like the old paper mill cases. Just because global carbon is a bigger, more complex problem than a single paper mill doesn't mean the state courts are suddenly incapable of doing their jobs. The policy fears do not override the legal reality.

After working through those arguments, the panel takes its final vote. It is nine to nothing to affirm the lower court. That means they agree with the Colorado Supreme Court. The state court has the legal right to hear Boulder's lawsuit, and federal law does not block it.

And that brings us to our prediction on the record. The real Supreme Court has not decided this case yet. The actual disposition is pending. So, the AI panel's nine to nothing vote is now a public, falsifiable prediction. They are putting their digital necks on the block. If the real Court decides to reverse the lower court and kick the case to federal court, our AI panel is dead wrong. I relish how exposed that position is. They have to live with this call, in public, until the real justices finally speak.

We have to be honest about our season scoreboard. The deliberating panel is currently sitting at sixty-six point seven percent correct. They are losing to the silent jury, which is at seventy-seven point four percent, and the solo model, which is at eighty-three percent. The deliberation process is actually hurting their accuracy this season. When they talk, they get distracted by the policy arguments and lose sight of the strict legal text.

It is a fascinating dynamic. The silent model just crunches the data and moves on without getting swayed by a persuasive speaker. But we will keep watching. The panel's rulings on all these pending cases are on the public record. We will see if their confidence holds up when the real Supreme Court finally hands down its decisions. I'm your court-watcher.
And I'm your legal-affairs correspondent. Thanks for listening to SPLIT DECISION.
